sections.title: What is modality? (1)
sections.content:
**Modality** is the way we express meanings such as possibility, necessity, permission, ability, obligation, advice, or intention – rather than stating a plain fact.

When we say *She **can** swim* or *You **must** go*, we are not just saying that something happens; we add a layer of meaning: ability (can), obligation (must). That layer is **modality**.

sections.title: What is modality? (2)
sections.content: In English we express modality mainly with **modal verbs** (can, must, should, will, etc.) and **semi-modals** (ought to, have to, need to, be able to). They work with the main verb and change its meaning. For example: *I go* is a fact; *I **might** go* adds possibility; *I **should** go* adds advice or weak obligation.

sections.title: Modal verbs – form and meaning (1)
sections.content:
The main **modal verbs** are: **can**, **could**, **may**, **might**, **must**, **shall**, **should**, **will**, **would**.

**Form:** They have no -s in the third person (*she can*, not *she cans*). They are followed by the **base form** of the verb (*can go*, *must see*). Questions and negatives are made without *do*: *Can you?*, *I must not*.

sections.title: Modal verbs – form and meaning (1)
sections.content:
**Main uses:**
• **can** – ability, permission (*I can swim*; *You can leave early*)
• **could** – past ability, polite request, possibility (*I could run fast when I was young*; *Could you help me?*)
• **may / might** – possibility, permission (*It may rain*; *May I sit here?*)

sections.title: Modal verbs – form and meaning (2)
sections.content:
• **must** – obligation, strong deduction (*You must wear a seatbelt*; *She must be at home*)
• **should** – advice, weak obligation (*You should see a doctor*)
• **will / would** – future, requests, conditionals (*I will call you*; *Would you pass the salt?*)

sections.title: Semi-modals (e.g. ought to) (1)
sections.content:
**Semi-modals** (or marginal modals) express similar meanings to modals but behave more like ordinary verbs: they may take -s, or be followed by **to** + infinitive.

• **Ought to** – advice or moral obligation (similar to *should*, often more formal or stronger): *You ought to apologise*. *She ought to see a doctor.* Form: **ought to** + base verb. Negative: *ought not to* or *oughtn't to*.

sections.title: Semi-modals (e.g. ought to) (1)
sections.content: • **Have to / have got to** – necessity or external obligation (*I have to go now*; *She has to wear a uniform*). Takes -s in 3rd person; *do* in questions/negatives: *Do I have to?*

sections.title: Semi-modals (e.g. ought to) (2)
sections.content:
• **Need to** – necessity (*You need to apply by Friday*). Behaves like a normal verb: *needs*, *don't need to*.

• **Be able to** – ability (used when *can* is not possible, e.g. future or other tenses): *She will be able to come.*

• **Used to** – past habit (*I used to play tennis*).

sections.title: Summary – when to use which (1)
sections.content:
• **Obligation:** *must* (strong, from the speaker), *have to* (often external), *ought to* / *should* (advice or weaker obligation).
• **Possibility:** *may*, *might*, *could* (*It might rain*).
• **Ability:** *can*, *could* (past), *be able to* (when you need another tense).
• **Permission:** *can*, *may*, *could* (polite).
• **Advice:** *should*, *ought to* (*You should / ought to rest*).

sections.title: Summary – when to use which (2)
sections.content:
Remember: modals have no -s and no *to* before the main verb (except *ought to*); semi-modals like *have to* and *ought to* use *to* + infinitive.

See the reference table on the next page.

questions.question: What is **modality** in grammar?

options.a: The time of the action (past, present, future)
options.b: The way we express possibility, necessity, permission, advice, etc.
options.c: The person (I, you, he/she) of the subject

questions.explanation: **Modality** is how we express meanings like possibility, necessity, permission, ability, obligation, or advice – the 'layer' of meaning added by modals and semi-modals.

questions.question: Which sentence uses a **semi-modal**?

options.b: You ought to see a doctor.

questions.explanation: **Ought to** is a semi-modal (followed by **to** + base verb). **Can**, **must**, and **might** are core modals (no *to*).

questions.question: 'You ____ apologise.' (strong advice, formal) Which fits best?
questions.explanation: **Ought to** expresses advice or moral obligation, often in a more formal tone. *Should* is also possible but *ought to* fits 'formal' best here.

questions.question: Which of these uses 'to' before the main verb? (e.g. ___ to go)
questions.explanation: **Ought to** always includes 'to' before the main verb: we say 'ought to go', 'ought to see'. **Must**, **should**, and **can** are followed directly by the base form (must go, should see, can go).

questions.question: For external obligation (e.g. a rule or someone else's requirement), we often use:
questions.explanation: **Have to** is commonly used for external obligation (rules, boss, law). **Must** can express the speaker's own obligation or a strong rule; **have to** is typical when the obligation comes from outside.

questions.question: Which verb do we use for **ability** when we need a form that *can* doesn't have (e.g. future)?
questions.explanation: **Be able to** is used for ability when we need another tense or structure, e.g. *She will be able to come*, *I have been able to finish*. **Can** has no future or perfect form.

gapfill.title: Practice: Choose the modal or semi-modal

questions.question: You ____ (advice) see a doctor if your headache continues. (ought to / should)
questions.explanation: Advice → **ought to** or **should** + base verb.

questions.question: She ____ speak three languages. (ability)
questions.explanation: Ability in the present → **can** + base verb.

questions.question: We ____ wear a uniform at school. (external rule)
questions.explanation: External obligation (school rule) → **have to** or **must**.

questions.question: It ____ rain later, so take an umbrella. (possibility)
questions.explanation: Possibility → **might** (or may/could) + base verb.

questions.question: You ____ (not) tell anyone – it's a secret. (obligation not to)
questions.explanation: Obligation not to do something → **must not** or **cannot**.

questions.question: He ____ to give up smoking. (advice, semi-modal)
questions.explanation: Advice with the semi-modal → **ought to** + base verb.
